text: Laparoscopic frontal mesh rectopexy is a gold standard treatment for rectal prolapse and yields very good functional results. Unfortunately, there are recurrences. In this video, we will present three cases, discuss tips, tricks and pitfalls based on laparoscopic footage.

The first case is in a leprous, fifty-nine-year-old female who had her first laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy for external rectal prolapse at the age of fifty. After being well for several years, she came back to the outpatient clinic with symptoms of recurrent external prolapse, which was facilitated by a major enterocele.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic procedure by temporarily retracting the uterus and sigmoid colon. The peritoneum is opened over the sacral promontory, exposing the anterior bony surface. The dissection continues as the peritoneum is opened towards the Douglas pouch, entering the vaginal septum and progressing onto the pelvic floor, while avoiding the incorporated mesh.

The surgeon positions and folds the mesh over the posterior aspect of the rectum, securing it with multiple interrupted partial thickness PDS-TRIO sutures over the rectal mesentery. The anterior part of the mesh is positioned and fixed onto the denuded aspect of the rectum. Both meshes are brought together and snugly fixed onto the sacral promontory. The peritoneum is closed to separate it from the abdominal cavity.

The second case is a young multiporous female of thirty-nine years old, who had a laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy three years earlier and reported recurrent symptoms of obstructive defecation, constipation, pelvic discomfort, The proctogram was inconclusive with respect to the recurrent symptoms. Therefore, a rectal examination under anesthesia was performed subsequently, which revealed a recurrent high-grade internal rectal prolapse and moderate-sized rectocele. The first impression is that the recurrence is due to insufficient fixation of the mesh onto the rectum.

The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, identifying a virgin plane left of the previous mesh. The dissection progresses towards the rectovaginal septum and pelvic floor, encountering a fibrous strand typical after prior mesh insertion. The mesh appears fixed only to the vagina, prompting the use of a vaginal retractor for additional space and traction. The deeper dissection reveals insufficient previous fixation in the rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon positions and folds the mesh over the anterior aspect of the rectum, securing it with sutures. The mesh is fixed onto the sacral promontory. A redundant portion of the mesh is resected. Due to limited vaginal prolapse, the vaginal apex is fixed onto the mesh using PDS-TRIO sutures before closing the peritoneum to cover the mesh.

The fixation of the sigmoid and uterus is removed. The surgeon begins dissection in the deep Douglas pouch by opening the peritoneum, which could reveal an enterocele contributing to obstructed defecation.

The surgeon dissects the rectovaginal septum to the left of the previously inserted mesh, focusing on the pelvic floor anteriorly. The dissection also includes the posterior plane due to the predominantly posterior external rectal prolapse. Given the patient's senior age and the extent of mobilization, a decision is made to use a single anteriorly placed mesh.

The surgeon positions and folds the new mesh over the anterior aspect of the rectum, suturing it onto the rectum with BDS-TRIO. The previous mesh remains visible, attached to the vagina, positioned to the right of the new mesh. The new mesh is tensioned and fixed onto the sacral promontory. The end result is checked, and additional fixation of the mesh onto the rectum is performed if necessary, before closing the peritoneum to envelop the mesh in the retroperitoneal space, preventing adhesion formation.

To summarize, laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is effective but not without failure, which might be caused by insufficient rectovaginal dissection, mesh detachment from the rectum, mesh detachment from the sacral promontory. Redo laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is feasible and appears to be safe. Our approach is to define the sacral promontory while working around the pre-existing mesh to ensure optimal placement and fixation onto the new mesh with a slow absorbable monofilament suture and synthetic or biological mesh.
